ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Vietnamese banh mi with grilled chicken; mapped to closest parent sandwich food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese banh mi; A0FKP (Sandwich) used as the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese banh mi sandwich; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole baguette sandwich weighing approximately 350 g, as commonly sold at Vietnamese sandwich stalls and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken sandwich with vegetables, on baguette-type roll (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database for Vietnamese-style sandwich analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Vietnamese banh mi ga nuong. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite data for grilled chicken sandwiches on white bread rolls, adjusted for the Vietnamese preparation profile (fish sauce marinade, pickled vegetables, fresh herbs). The input record values (energy 157.14 kcal, protein 8.57 g, fat 7.14 g, carbohydrate 14.29 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Sodium at 342.86 mg/100 g reflects the fish sauce and soy sauce marinade contribution. All values cross-checked for plausibility against HPB Singapore food composition data for similar sandwich-type dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread / baguette-type roll analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese banh mi ga nuong. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the white French baguette base, which is the dominant carbohydrate source. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 report GI of white bread / baguette-type rolls at approximately 70–75. The grilled chicken, pickled vegetables, and fat content will modestly attenuate the glycaemic response in practice, but the composite dish GI is conservatively estimated at 70. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 50.0) / 100 = 35.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 14.29 × 3.5 = 50.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 342.86 mg/100 g (approximately 1200 mg per 350 g serving) driven by fish sauce, soy sauce, and optional pâté in the marinade and condiments.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a sandwich, request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce in the marinade, omit pâté, and choose low-sodium condiment alternatives to keep sodium intake within the 600 mg per meal target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white baguette base (GI ~70) with 14.29 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.14 g dietary fibre per 100 g results in rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 50 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a wholegrain or multigrain roll substitute to lower the GI, limit the serving to half a sandwich, increase the proportion of pickled vegetables and cucumber to add fibre, and pair the meal with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.29 g/100 g (approximately 8 g per serving) and sodium at 342.86 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; trans fat is present at 0.06 g/100 g, and optional pâté further elevates saturated fat and sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should omit pâté and mayonnaise to reduce saturated fat and sodium, choose a wholegrain roll to improve the fibre and glycaemic profile, and limit the serving to half a sandwich to keep saturated fat below 5 g and sodium below 600 mg per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pâté is a processed high-saturated-fat product; chilli is a nightshade ingredient that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals; the dish lacks omega-3 sources and meaningful anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should omit pâté and chilli, substitute avocado for a healthier fat profile, add ginger or turmeric to the marinade for anti-inflammatory benefit, and reduce fish sauce and soy sauce to lower sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Mì Gà Nướng (Baguette with Grilled Chicken Chop) is a Vietnamese-style baguette sandwich featuring marinated grilled chicken chop as the primary protein, served inside a crisp French baguette roll. The chicken is typically marinated in a blend of lemongrass, fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and sugar before being grilled to produce a caramelised, smoky exterior with a juicy interior. The sandwich is filled with fresh cucumber slices, pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), fresh coriander (cilantro), sliced chilli, and optionally pâté and mayonnaise. The combination of textures — crispy baguette crust, tender grilled chicken, crunchy pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs — is characteristic of the bánh mì style. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 157 kcal per 100 g, with 8.57 g protein, 7.14 g fat, and 14.29 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 342.86 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and soy sauce in the marinade. A full 350 g serving delivers approximately 550 kcal, 30 g protein, 25 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate. The high-GI white baguette base (GI ~70) makes this dish a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The dish is popular in Singapore as a Vietnamese street-food-inspired meal available through food delivery platforms and Vietnamese sandwich stalls. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains gluten, soy, and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese banh mi ga nuong exists in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA composite data for grilled chicken sandwiches on white bread rolls, adjusted for the Vietnamese preparation profile. The input record values are consistent with this composite and have been retained. Confidence is medium because the values are plausible and cross-checked but not derived from a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Vietnamese-style grilled chicken baguette are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database as of 2024 for this specific composite dish. trans_fat_g is reported at 0.06 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with trace levels in the baguette and marinade components. Duplicate portion entries in the input record (two identical '1 baguette sandwich' entries) have been consolidated to a single canonical serving of 350 g. The dish is classified as halal-suitable assuming standard halal-certified chicken and fish sauce are used; pâté may not be halal and should be confirmed with the vendor. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the white French baguette base per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); the actual composite GI may be slightly lower due to the protein and fat content of the filling. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (70 × 50.0) / 100 = 35.0 g. Chinese names (simplified: 烤鸡法棍三明治; traditional: 烤雞法棍三明治), Malay name (Baguette dengan Ayam Panggang), Tamil name (வறுத்த கோழி பாகெட் சாண்ட்விச்), and Hindi name (ग्रिल्ड चिकन बैगेट सैंडविच) added to satisfy Singaporean multilingual naming requirements, as this dish is sold in Singapore. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to conform to allowed values.
